1: וַיִּקְרָא יִצְחָק אֶל-יַעֲקֹב וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתוֹ — "Isaac called Jacob and blessed him." Despite the deception of Genesis 27, Isaac now willingly and knowingly blesses Jacob — the second deliberate blessing from Isaac to Jacob, contrasting with the first (given unwittingly under deception). The explicit endorse of Jacob's departure to Paddan-Aram and the command not to marry a Canaanite woman formally authorize the mission. Isaac's cooperation with God's plan (Genesis 25:23: the elder will serve the younger) is now conscious, even if it was unconscious in Genesis 27. לֹא-תִקַּח אִשָּׁה מִבְּנוֹת כְּנָעַן (do not take a wife from the daughters of Canaan) — the prohibition echoes Abraham's command to his servant in Genesis 24:3; the covenant endogamy principle is transmitted from Abraham to Isaac to Jacob as explicit instruction.
2: פַּדֶּנָה אֲרָם (paddennāh ʾărām, Paddan-Aram) — "Paddan-Aram" (= the plain of Aram or the field/road of Aram — paddan possibly from Aramaic paddānāʾ, field, or Hebrew pāḏāh-related; it is the region around Haran in northwestern Mesopotamia, synonymous with ʾĂram naharāyim of Genesis 24). Jacob is being sent to the same region the servant traveled in Genesis 24 — the Nahorite homeland where Rebekah came from. מִבְּנוֹת לָבָן אֲחִי אִמֶּךָ (from the daughters of Laban, your mother's brother) — the kinship connection is specific: Laban's daughters, not merely any Aramean woman. The narrative is already preparing for Rachel and Leah (Genesis 29).
3: וְאֵל שַׁדַּי יְבָרֵךְ אֹתְךָ (wĕʾēl šadday yĕḇārēḵ ʾōtĕḵā) — "May El Shaddai bless you." ʾĒl Šadday (El Shaddai) — the divine name used by God in his initial covenant with Abraham (Genesis 17:1: "I am El Shaddai; walk before me and be blameless"). The name appears here for the second time in Genesis (cf. Genesis 17:1; repeated at Genesis 35:11; Genesis 43:14; Genesis 48:3; and throughout Job). Its precise meaning is debated: (1) "God Almighty" (traditional rendering; LXX: παντοκράτωρ); (2) "God of the mountain/highland" (from šad, mountain — cognate to Akkadian šadû); (3) "God of the field/wilderness" (from šāḏeh). The connection to the covenant-making context (Genesis 17:1) suggests a formal, solemn divine name for the multiplication-and-possession promise. Isaac invokes this name specifically for the multiplication blessing that follows.
4: וְיִתֶּן-לְךָ אֶת-בִּרְכַּת אַבְרָהָם (wĕyittĕn-lĕḵā ʾet-birkat ʾaḇrāhām) — "may he give you the blessing of Abraham." The explicit transmission: this is the blessing of Abraham — the covenant blessing of Genesis 12:2-3; Genesis 17:1-8; Genesis 22:17-18 — now formally declared as Jacob's inheritance. לְרִשְׁתְּךָ אֶת-אֶרֶץ מְגֻרֶיךָ (that you may take possession of the land of your sojournings) — gûr (to sojourn, dwell as a resident alien) — the land where the patriarchs have lived as sojourners (mĕgûrîm) will become the land of possession (môrāšāh). The sojourner's land becomes inheritance — the transformation that the covenant promises.
5: וַיִּשְׁלַח יִצְחָק אֶת-יַעֲקֹב (wayyišlaḥ yiṣḥāq ʾet-yaʿăqōḇ) — "Isaac sent Jacob." The send-verb (šālaḥ) again — now it is Isaac who formally sends Jacob, transforming the flight from Esau into an authorized patriarchal mission. Lāḵet paddennāh ʾărāmāh (to go to Paddan-Aram) — the destination is established with legal formality. The narrative adds the full genealogical identification of Laban: Bethuel's son, Rebekah's brother, Jacob and Esau's mother's brother — layering in all the family connections that make the destination the right one.
6: Esau observes Isaac's blessing of Jacob and the explicit prohibition against Canaanite wives. The verb wayyarʾ ʿēśāw (Esau saw) — perception leading to action, as in Genesis 3:6 (Eve saw the tree) and Genesis 6:2 (the sons of God saw the daughters of men). What Esau sees prompts a correction in his marriage strategy (Genesis 28:8-9). The narrative gives Esau credit for trying to please his father: he takes note of what displeases Isaac and attempts to rectify it — though the rectification (marrying Ishmael's daughter) is too late and beside the point of the birthright/blessing crisis.
7: שָׁמַע יַעֲקֹב אֶל-אָבִיו וְאֶל-אִמּוֹ (šāmaʿ yaʿăqōḇ ʾel-ʾāḇîw wĕʾel-ʾimmô) — "Jacob obeyed his father and his mother." Šāmaʿ ʾel (to hear/obey toward) — the syntax of obedience; Jacob is observed by Esau as obedient to both parents. The irony is multilayered: Jacob obeyed Rebekah's plan in Genesis 27 (which deceived Isaac) and is now obeying Isaac's command (which Rebekah arranged); both acts of obedience are described with the same verb. Jacob's relationship with authority is complex: he obeys but within a web of family deception.
8: כִּי רָעוֹת בְּנוֹת כְּנַעַן בְּעֵינֵי יִצְחָק אָבִיו (kî rāʿôt bĕnôt kĕnaʿan bĕʿênê yiṣḥāq ʾāḇîw) — "the daughters of Canaan were displeasing in the eyes of his father Isaac." Rāʿôt (evil, bad, displeasing — plural) + the idiom bĕʿênê (in the eyes of) = the standard formula of moral evaluation. Esau now understands what he apparently did not grasp when he took his Hittite wives (Genesis 26:34-35): his father Isaac is opposed to Canaanite marriages on principle. The realization comes too late to undo his prior marriages; he can only add to them by marrying within a group Isaac might approve.
9: וַיֵּלֶךְ עֵשָׂו אֶל-יִשְׁמָעֵאל — "Esau went to Ishmael." The marriage to Mahalath, Ishmael's daughter, is Esau's attempt to correct his marriage record by adding a wife from Abraham's family line (though through the non-covenant branch). Maḥălat bat-yišmāʿēʾl ben-ʾaḇrāhām — the full identification establishes the Abrahamic connection Esau is leveraging. ʿAl-nāšāyw (in addition to his wives) — the addition of Mahalath does not remove the displeasing Hittite wives; Esau's correction is additive, not remedial. The gesture reveals Esau's sincere desire to please his father, even if inadequately executed.
10: וַיֵּצֵא יַעֲקֹב מִבְּאֵר שָׁבַע וַיֵּלֶךְ חָרָנָה (wayyēṣēʾ yaʿăqōḇ mibbĕʾēr šāḇaʿ wayyēleḵ ḥārānāh) — "Jacob went out from Beersheba and walked toward Haran." The departure formula: he goes out (yāṣāʾ) from the covenant settlement and walks (hālaḵ) toward the ancestral homeland. Bĕʾēr šāḇaʿ (Beersheba) — the site of Abraham's covenant with Abimelech (Genesis 21:31) and the place where YHWH appeared to Isaac (Genesis 26:24-25); the departure from Beersheba is departure from the established covenant geography. Jacob takes nothing with him — no caravan, no camels, no servants, no dowry gifts — in striking contrast with the servant's mission in Genesis 24 (ten camels loaded with gifts).
11: וַיִּפְגַּע בַּמָּקוֹם וַיָּלֶן שָׁם (wayyipgaʿ bammāqôm wayyālen šām) — "he encountered the place and spent the night there." Pāgaʿ (to meet, encounter, fall upon — often of encounters with persons: meeting an enemy, or of intercession, encountering God; cf. Isaiah 53:12 where the Servant pāgaʿ — intercedes for transgressors). The verb suggests an unplanned encounter: Jacob stumbles upon "the place" (hāmāqôm, the definite article implies either a known sacred site or the narrative's emphasis on its significance). The stone under his head is simply a headrest for the night; the holiness of the location is unknown to him until the dream. Kî-bāʾ haššemeš (because the sun had set) — the nightfall is the occasion for the dream; darkness is the setting for divine revelation.
12: וַיַּחֲלֹם וְהִנֵּה סֻלָּם מֻצָּב אַרְצָה וְרֹאשׁוֹ מַגִּיעַ הַשָּׁמָיְמָה — "he dreamed, and behold, a stairway set upon the earth with its top reaching to the heavens." Sullām — a hapax legomenon (appears only here in the OT); related to the root sāḵal (to raise up a road/ramp) or possibly Akkadian simmiltu (staircase, ramp — the type of processional ramp built into Mesopotamian ziggurats for cultic processions). The "ladder" translation (KJV, following LXX κλίμαξ) implies a climbing device; the Hebrew likely indicates a broad ramp or stairway suitable for processional traffic. וּמַלְאֲכֵי אֱלֹהִים עֹלִים וְיֹרְדִים בּוֹ (and angels of God ascending and descending on it) — the direction order is significant: angels ascend first, then descend — they are already present on earth at the start of the vision, going up to report and coming down commissioned. John 1:51 cites this vision in the mouth of Jesus: "you will see heaven opened and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man."
13: אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם אָבִיךָ וֵאלֹהֵי יִצְחָק — "I am the LORD, the God of your father Abraham and the God of Isaac." The divine self-identification formula: YHWH names himself by covenant relationship — not "God of heaven and earth" (the universal address of Genesis 24:3, 7) but "God of Abraham and Isaac." This is Jacob's first direct encounter with YHWH; the God who spoke to Abraham at Haran (Genesis 12:1), at Bethel (Genesis 12:7-8), and elsewhere now speaks to Jacob at this same Bethel location. הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר אַתָּה שֹׁכֵב עָלֶיהָ (the land on which you are lying) — the covenant promise is given in the most immediate terms: this very piece of ground under Jacob's sleeping body is part of the promised land.
14: וּפָרַצְתָּ יָמָּה וָקֵדְמָה וְצָפֹנָה וָנֶגְבָּה — "you will spread out to the west and east, north and south." Pāraṣ (to break out, burst through, spread rapidly — the word for the violent breaking down of a barrier; cf. Exodus 19:22: "lest the LORD break out against them"; 2 Samuel 5:20: David "broke out against his enemies like a bursting flood"). The four directions of spreading imply the promise is for total territorial dominion — the offspring will break through every boundary. וְנִבְרְכוּ בְךָ כָּל-מִשְׁפְּחֹת הָאֲדָמָה (and all the families/clans of the earth will be blessed through you) — the universal blessing formula of Genesis 12:3 (Niphal passive: they will be blessed through you) is here given to Jacob, completing the transmission: Abraham → Isaac → Jacob receives the universal blessing-promise.
15: וְהִנֵּה אָנֹכִי עִמָּךְ (wĕhinnēh ʾānōḵî ʿimmāḵ) — "and behold, I am with you." The divine promise of presence — ʿimmĕḵā (with you) — the core assurance of the covenant. This formula will become the structural backbone of covenant faithfulness throughout the OT: YHWH with Moses (Exodus 3:12), YHWH with Joshua (Joshua 1:5, 9), YHWH with Gideon (Judges 6:12-16), YHWH with Jeremiah (Jeremiah 1:8, 19). וּשְׁמַרְתִּיךָ בְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר-תֵּלֵךְ (and I will guard/keep you in all that you go) — šāmar (to keep, guard, preserve) — the same word used in the Aaronic blessing (Numbers 6:24: "may YHWH bless you and keep you"). לֹא אֶעֱזָבְךָ (I will not abandon you) — ʿāzaḇ (to abandon, forsake) — the negative formulation of covenant fidelity: YHWH will not do what Esau fears and what Jacob's own experience of being driven from home might suggest. Cited in Hebrews 13:5.
16: אָכֵן יֵשׁ יְהוָה בַּמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה וְאָנֹכִי לֹא יָדָעְתִּי (ʾāḵēn yēš YHWH bammāqôm hazzeh wĕʾānōḵî lōʾ yāḏāʿtî) — "surely YHWH is in this place, and I did not know it." ʾĀḵēn (surely, truly — an affirmative asseveration; cf. Job 1:10: "has not YHWH put a hedge around him?"; the word implies: despite contrary appearances, here is the truth). Jacob's theological discovery: the ordinary stopping place, chosen because the sun set and he was tired, is a place of divine presence. The OT theology of divine omnipresence emerges from these chance encounters: YHWH is not confined to the altar at Beersheba or the covenant location at Hebron — he meets Jacob in a field, at night, with a stone for a pillow.
17: מַה-נֹּורָא הַמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה (mah-nôrāʾ hāmāqôm hazzeh) — "how awesome/fear-inducing is this place!" Nôrāʾ — Niphal participle of yārēʾ (to fear/be afraid): "that which causes fear, that which is awe-inspiring." The same word is used for YHWH's deeds at the exodus (Exodus 34:10) and in the worship vocabulary of the Psalms (Psalms 65:5; Genesis 66:3, 5; Genesis 68:35; Genesis 96:4). Jacob's fear is not terror but the recognition of holiness — the appropriate response to unexpected divine encounter. בֵּית אֱלֹהִים (bêt ʾĔlōhîm, house of God) — the definition of what this place is: a bêt ʾĔlōhîm, anticipating the name Bethel. וְזֶה שַׁעַר הַשָּׁמָיִם (and this is the gate of heaven) — šaʿar (gate) — the civic/legal significance of a gate transferred to cosmic geography: where the sullām touches earth, the gate of heaven stands. This is the point of access between the human and the divine; the vertical axis of the universe passes here.
18: וַיַּשְׁכֵּם יַעֲקֹב בַּבֹּקֶר (wayyaškem yaʿăqōḇ babbōqer) — "Jacob rose early in the morning." Hiškîm (to rise early, the dawn-rising) — the same word as Abraham's early rising for the Akedah (Genesis 22:3) and the servant's early rising in Gerar (Genesis 20:8). Early rising marks urgency and responsiveness to the previous night's divine encounter. וַיַּצֵּב אֹתָהּ מַצֵּבָה (wayyaṣṣēḇ ʾōtāh maṣṣēḇāh) — "he set it up as a standing stone/pillar." Maṣṣēḇāh (standing stone, commemorative pillar — from nāṣaḇ, to stand firm; cf. Genesis 31:13, 45, 51-52 where Jacob and Laban also erect pillars) — a cultic memorial object marking a theophanic encounter. The later law (Deuteronomy 16:22: "you shall not erect a maṣṣēḇāh, for the LORD your God hates it") reflects a later prohibition against such standing stones when they had become associated with Canaanite fertility cult; here the practice is presented without condemnation as an appropriate memorial to theophany. Wayyiṣṣōq šemen ʿal-rōšāh (he poured oil on its top) — the anointing of the stone consecrates it as a sacred object and marker.
19: וַיִּקְרָא אֶת-שֵׁם-הַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא בֵּית-אֵל — "he called the name of that place Beth-El." Bêt-ʾēl = "house of God" (bêt = house + ʾēl = God) — the name directly expresses Jacob's theological experience: this is where he encountered God's house (Genesis 28:17). Wĕʾûlām lûz šem-hāʿîr lāriʾšōnāh (but the former name of the city was Luz) — the editorial note marks the transition: an ancient Canaanite city named Luz (possibly "almond tree" or related to Ugaritic lz, "to turn aside") receives a new Hebrew name encoding Israel's theological encounter. Bethel will become one of the most important sacred sites in Israel's history (cf. Genesis 35:6-15; Joshua 8:9; Judges 20:18, 26; 1 Samuel 7:16; 1 Kings 12:28-29 where Jeroboam establishes the golden calves there — the site's significance is its downfall).
20: וַיִּדַּר יַעֲקֹב נֶדֶר לֵאמֹר (wayyiddār yaʿăqōḇ neḏer lēʾmōr) — "Jacob made a vow, saying." Nāḏar (to vow, make a conditional pledge) — the standard OT vow formula; neḏer (vow) is the noun. The OT vow is a conditional promise to God: "if you do X, I will do Y." This structure is the standard form of the pilgrimage vow (cf. Num 30; Deuteronomy 23:21-23; Judges 11:30-31 — Jephthah's tragic vow; 1 Samuel 1:11 — Hannah's vow; Psalms 22:25 — fulfilling vows in the assembly). The conditionality of Jacob's vow ("if God will be with me... then YHWH will be my God") should be read as a standard vow form, not as Jacob setting terms for his relationship with God. He is framing a pledge in the standard conditional idiom of vow-making.
21: וְשַׁבְתִּי בְשָׁלוֹם אֶל-בֵּית אָבִי (wĕšaḇtî ḇĕšālôm ʾel-bêt ʾāḇî) — "and I return in peace to my father's house." Šālôm (peace, wholeness, safety) — the condition of the vow: safe return. וְהָיָה יְהוָה לִי לֵאלֹהִים (then YHWH will be my God) — the vow's pledge: covenantal loyalty to YHWH in return for divine protection and provision. The relationship Jacob is pledging is not merely acknowledgment of YHWH's existence but a covenantal claim: "YHWH will be lî (to me, for me) as God." The lî (for me, mine) carries the covenantal intimacy of "my God" (Exodus 15:2: "this is my God and I will praise him").
22: וְהָאֶבֶן הַזֹּאת אֲשֶׁר-שַׂמְתִּי מַצֵּבָה יִהְיֶה בֵּית אֱלֹהִים — "and this stone that I have set up as a pillar will be God's house." The pillar-stone becomes the material anchor of Bethel's sacred geography: what was just a random stone becomes the marker of "God's house." The vow transforms a provisional camping stone into a permanent theological monument. וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר תִּתֶּן-לִי עַשֵּׂר אֲעַשְּׂרֶנּוּ לָךְ (wĕḵōl ʾăšer tittĕn-lî ʿaśśēr ʾăʿaśśĕrennû lāḵ) — "and of all you give me I will surely tithe a tenth to you." The emphatic Piel infinitive absolute ʿaśśēr ʾăʿaśśĕrennû (I will surely tithe a tithe) — the pattern of absolute dedication: 10% of all divine provision returns to God. This is the first explicit tithe vow in the OT (Abraham's tithe in 14:20 was a voluntary post-victory gift; this is a forward-looking covenant pledge). The tithe at Bethel establishes the pattern that will be codified in Leviticus 27:30-33; Numbers 18:21-32; Deuteronomy 14:22-29; Malachi 3:10.
